q:
What is correct?
The direction indicator must be switched on …

A. ... when leaving a roundabout.
B. ... when entering a roundabout.
C. ... when leaving or entering a roundabout.
e:
LESSON 19 – ‘Junction and traffic sign’.
You leave a roundabout: it must burn.
You want to enter a roundabout: it may burn.
clean_q:
What is correct?
The direction indicator must be switched on … 
A. ... when leaving a roundabout. 
B. ... when entering a roundabout. 
C. ... when leaving or entering a roundabout.
clean_e:
LESSON 19 – ‘Junction and traffic sign’.  
You leave a roundabout: it must burn.
You want to enter a roundabout: it may burn.

q:
This driver wants to turn right at this junction.
No other road users are visible.
Must he switch on the right indicator?

A. Yes, it’s always required.
B. No, if there’s no one around, it’s pointless.
e:
LESSON 21
Every lateral movement you make must be signalled using the indicator.
It doesn’t matter whether there are other drivers or not.

This driver wants to turn right at this junction.

B. No, if there’s no one around, it’s pointless.
clean_e:
LESSON 21
Every lateral movement you make must be signalled using the indicator.
It doesn’t matter whether there are other drivers or not.

q:
Which traffic sign is associated with the road marking called shark’s teeth?

A. With sign 1.
B. With sign 2.
C. With sign 3.
e:
LESSON 19
An inverted triangle means you must give way to drivers on the road you are about to enter and stop if necessary.
If there are no drivers on the crossing road, you don’t have to stop and may enter the intersection.
A line formed by inverted triangles, also known as shark’s teeth, indicates the place where you must stop if necessary.
If it’s not really necessary, you don’t have to make a full stop.

A line formed by inverted triangles, also known as shark’s teeth, indicates the place where you must stop if necessary.
If it’s not really necessary, you don’t have to make a full stop.

q:
This driver wants to turn left at this junction.
Even if there are no other road users, the driver must first come to a complete stop before entering the junction.

A. True.
B. False.
e:
LESSON 19
The give-way sign (inverted triangle) means that you must yield to other drivers and, if necessary, come to a complete stop.
If there are no other drivers, you don’t need to stop completely and may enter the junction.
If there is a STOP sign, however, you must come to a complete stop.

q:
Fill in a number.
After which traffic sign are you driving on a priority road?
e:
LESSON 19
Sign 2 means you are on a priority road. You have priority at all intersections until the sign is cancelled or another sign changes the priority.
Sign 1 gives you priority only at the next intersection.
Sign 3 says nothing about priority; it only means traffic flows in one direction.
Sign 4 simply indicates a direction you must follow, not priority.

Sign 1 gives you priority only at the next intersection.
Sign 3 says nothing about priority; it only means traffic flows in one direction.
Sign 4 simply indicates a direction you must follow, not priority.

q:
At what maximum speed may this car drive past the yellow sign within a built-up area in the Brussels Region?

A. 30 km/h.
B. 50 km/h.
C. 70 km/h.
e:
LESSON 12
It is a roadway within a built-up area.
In the Walloon and Flemish Regions, the maximum permitted speed inside built-up areas is 50 km/h.
In the Brussels Region, it is 30 km/h.
The yellow sign has nothing to do with speed — it is a priority-road sign indicating that you are driving on a priority road.

q:
You are approaching this intersection. Where must you stop?

A. Just before the stop line.
B. 1 metre before the stop line.
C. Just before the STOP sign.
e:
LESSON 19
You must always come to a complete stop when there is a STOP sign, even if there are no other road users. You stop just before the stop line. If there is no stop line, you stop level with the STOP sign. You must give way to other drivers on the road you intend to enter.

q:
The driver of this car saw no other road users and carefully entered the intersection without stopping.
Was he allowed to do that?

A. Yes, because there is no stop line on the road.
B. No, he must stop first.
e:
LESSON 19
A STOP means you must come to a complete stop at the stop line and yield to drivers on the public road you want to enter.
If there is no stop line on the pavement, you must stop at the traffic sign.
Even if there are no drivers on the crossing road, you must still come to a complete stop before entering the intersection.

q:
You are driving in the inner lane of a two-lane roundabout. What must you do to exit?

A. Stop and wait until the cars in the outer lane have passed.
B. Exit directly, you have priority over the cars in the outer lane.
C. Keep circulating until the outer lane is free so you can exit.
e:
LESSON 19
To exit a two-lane roundabout from the inner lane, you must first change lanes to the outer lane, using your right indicator. You must give way to vehicles already driving in the outer lane. If you cannot do this in time, it is safer to make another loop around the roundabout rather than blocking traffic or creating a dangerous situation.
clean_q:

clean_e:
LESSON 19
To exit a two-lane roundabout from the inner lane, you must first change lanes to the outer lane, using your right indicator. You must give way to vehicles already driving in the outer lane. If you cannot do this in time, it is safer to make another loop around the roundabout rather than blocking traffic or creating a dangerous situation.

The car must give priority to MANAGERS on the intersection.
A pedestrian is not a driver, but there is a crossroads for pedestrians.
Therefore the pedestrian is allowed to go first.
clean_q:
Who is the first to cross the intersection?
A. The pedestrian.
B. The car.
clean_e:
LESSON 19 – ‘Junction and traffic sign’.  
The car must give priority to MANAGERS on the intersection.
A pedestrian is not a driver, but there is a crossroads for pedestrians.
Therefore the pedestrian is allowed to go first.

q:
A roundabout.
When must you switch on the right indicator?

A. When entering a roundabout.
B. When leaving a roundabout.
C. When entering and leaving a roundabout.
e:
LESSON 19
When leaving a roundabout, you must switch on the right indicator.
clean_q:
A roundabout.
When must you switch on the right indicator?
A. When entering a roundabout.
B. When leaving a roundabout.
C. When entering and leaving a roundabout.
clean_e:
LESSON 19
When leaving a roundabout, you must switch on the right indicator.

q:
When you see this traffic sign, you know that you …

A. ... must stop.
B. ... have priority.
C. ... have to give priority and if necessary stop.
e:
LESSON 19 – ‘Junction and traffic sign’.
Meaning: Provide priority and, if necessary, stop.
Type: Priority board.
clean_q:
When you see this traffic sign, you know that you … 
A. ... must stop.
B. ... have priority.
C. ... have to give priority and if necessary stop.
clean_e:
LESSON 19 – ‘Junction and traffic sign’.  
Meaning: Provide priority and, if necessary, stop.
Type: Priority board.

q:
Who is allowed to cross first at the junction?

A. Car A.
B. Car B.
e:
LESSON 19 – ‘Junction and traffic sign’.
Next to car A is a stop sign.
clean_q:
Who is allowed to cross first at the junction? 
A. Car A.
B. Car B.
clean_e:
LESSON 19 – ‘Junction and traffic sign’.  
Next to car A is a stop sign.

q:
Who is allowed to cross first at the junction?

A. Car A.
B. Car B.
e:
LESSON 19 – ‘Junction and traffic sign’.
The traffic sign at the right of car A says that the driver has priority at this intersection.
clean_q:
Who is allowed to cross first at the junction? 
A. Car A.
B. Car B.
clean_e:
LESSON 19 – ‘Junction and traffic sign’.  
The traffic sign at the right of car A says that the driver has priority at this intersection.
